sentences.base_language: Haeun had made a reservation at a new restaurant that everyone in her circle had been talking about.
sentences.target_language: 하은은 주변 사람들이 모두 이야기하던 새 레스토랑에 예약을 해 두었다.
sentences.romanized: Haeun-eun jubyeon saramdeul-i modu iyagihadeun sae reseutorang-e yeyak-eul hae dueotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-던** retrospective modifier: 이야기하**던** 레스토랑 (the restaurant that [people] had been talking about). **-던** attaches to a verb stem and modifies a noun with a sense of past habit or incomplete/ongoing past action: 살**던** 동네 (the neighbourhood where [I] used to live), 먹**던** 음식 (the food [I] was eating). It differs from **-(으)ㄴ** (simple past completion): 이야기한 레스토랑 would mean a restaurant that was talked about (once, completely), whereas 이야기하**던** emphasises the ongoing buzz.
**-아/어 두다** preparatory resultant state: 해 **두었다** (had made and left it in place). **-아/어 두다** signals that an action is performed in advance so that its result is ready for later use: 준비해 **두다** (prepare in advance), 사 **두다** (buy ahead of time). It adds a planning dimension — the reservation was made earlier precisely so that tonight's dinner could happen.

sentences.base_language: When they arrived, the restaurant was beautifully decorated with warm lighting and already nearly full.
sentences.target_language: 그들이 도착했을 때, 레스토랑은 따뜻한 조명으로 아름답게 꾸며져 있었고 이미 거의 자리가 찼다.
sentences.romanized: Geudeul-i dochaghaeosseul ttae, reseutorang-eun ttatteuthan jomyeong-euro areumdapge kkumyeojyeo isseotgo imi geoui jari-ga chatda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어져 있다** passive resultant state: 꾸며져 **있었고** (was decorated — in the decorated state). **꾸미다** (to decorate) → **꾸며지다** (to be decorated — passive) + **-어 있다** (resultant state): the restaurant had been decorated and remained in that state upon their arrival. Compare: **꾸며졌다** (was decorated — the event), **꾸며져 있었다** (was in a decorated state — ongoing result).
**-았/었을 때** specific past temporal clause: 도착**했을 때** (when they had arrived). The past modifier form **-았/었을** specifies a particular past moment: 도착**할 때** would be general or future ('when arriving'), while 도착**했을 때** pins the scene to the specific moment of their arrival. This is a key distinction for narrative precision in Korean.

sentences.base_language: A friendly staff member greeted them and led them to a great table by the window.
sentences.target_language: 친절한 직원이 그들을 반갑게 맞이하고는 창가의 좋은 자리로 안내해 주었다.
sentences.romanized: Chinjeolhan jigwon-i geudeul-eul bangapge majihaegoneun changga-eui joeun jari-ro annaehaejueotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-고는** sequential with shift of focus: 맞이하**고는** 안내했다 (greeted and then led). **-고는** follows an action and signals that a subsequent, often contrasting or consequential action follows. It carries a nuance of 'after doing X, [something else then happened]': 들어오**고는** 바로 앉았다 (came in and then sat right down). The **는** adds a mild emphasis or topicalising sense to the first action before moving to the next.
**안내해 주다** = to guide/show the way (as a service): **안내해 주었다** (led them, did the favour of showing them). **안내하다** (to guide/escort) + **-아/어 주다** (benefactive): the staff member performed the guiding for the guests' benefit. **-아/어 주다** is used extensively in service contexts to highlight consideration for the other party: 설명해 **주다** (explain for someone), 가져다 **주다** (bring for someone).

sentences.base_language: Haeun felt immediately at ease — the atmosphere was refined without being uncomfortable.
sentences.target_language: 하은은 곧바로 편안함을 느꼈다. 분위기가 고급스러우면서도 전혀 부담스럽지 않았다.
sentences.romanized: Haeun-eun gotbaro pyeonanham-eul neukkkyeotda. Bunwigi-ga gogeupseureouмyeonseodo jeonhyeo budamseureopji anhatda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-면서도** simultaneous concession: 고급스러우**면서도** 부담스럽지 않았다 (refined, yet not uncomfortable). **-면서도** combines the simultaneity of **-면서** (while) with the concessive force of **-도** (even/yet): both qualities exist at the same time — elegant and yet relaxed. Compare: **-지만** (but — sequential contrast), **-면서도** (while at the same time — simultaneous contrast). This pattern elegantly captures two coexisting but seemingly contradictory attributes.
**-지 않다** negation: 부담스럽**지 않았다** (was not burdensome). **-지 않다** is the standard verb negation pattern in Korean, attaching directly to the verb/adjective stem: 좋**지 않다** (is not good), 오**지 않다** (does not come). It is interchangeable with 안 + verb in most contexts but more emphatic and natural with descriptive verbs in formal registers: 편하**지 않다** vs 안 편하다.

sentences.base_language: The menu was extensive, with carefully composed dishes using seasonal Korean ingredients.
sentences.target_language: 메뉴는 방대했고, 제철 한국 식재료를 활용한 정성스럽게 구성된 요리들이 가득했다.
sentences.romanized: Menyu-neun bangdaehaetgo,jecheol hanguk sikjaeryo-reul hwallyonghan jeongseongseureobge guseongdoen yoriideul-i gadeukhaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄴ** past modifier on action verbs: 활용**한** 식재료 (ingredients that were utilized), 구성**된** 요리 (dishes that were composed). The past modifier **-(으)ㄴ** turns an action verb into an attributive describing the noun's past relationship to the action: 만**든** 음식 (food that was made), 쓴 편지 (a letter that was written). When stacked — 활용**한** … 구성**된** 요리들 — the effect is a richly modified noun phrase typical of formal Korean prose.
**가득하다** = to be full of, to be plentiful: 요리들이 **가득했다** (the dishes were plentiful / the menu was full of dishes). **가득** (full, brimful) + **하다** forms an adjective: 희망으로 **가득하다** (full of hope), 사람들로 **가득하다** (full of people). Compare: **많다** (to be many/much — quantity), **가득하다** (to be full of — conveys abundance filling a space or category).

sentences.base_language: She decided on the chef's tasting course and asked for a drink recommendation.
sentences.target_language: 하은은 셰프의 테이스팅 코스로 하기로 하고, 어울리는 음료를 추천해 달라고 부탁했다.
sentences.romanized: Haeun-eun syepeu-eui teiseutiing koseu-ro hagiро hago, eoulllineun eumnyo-reul chucheonhae dallago butakhaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-기로 하다** decision: 테이스팅 코스로 하**기로 하고** (decided to go with the tasting course). **-기로 하다** marks a deliberate choice or resolution, attaching to the verb nominaliser **-기**: 이쪽으로 가**기로 했다** (decided to go this way). When followed by **-고** it chains the decision naturally into the next action — she decided, and then asked.
**-아/어 달라고 하다** indirect request quotation: 추천해 **달라고** 부탁했다 (asked [them] to recommend). **달라고** is the quotative form of **달라** (the imperative/request form of **주다** directed toward the speaker: 'give me'). Indirect requests to a third party use **-아/어 달라고**: 가르쳐 **달라고** 했다 (asked [someone] to teach [me]). Compare: **-아/어 주라고** (indirect request directing a favour toward a third person, not the speaker).

sentences.base_language: The staff member suggested a traditional makgeolli pairing that she said complemented the seafood perfectly.
sentences.target_language: 직원은 해산물과 완벽하게 어울린다고 하면서, 전통 막걸리와의 페어링을 추천해 주었다.
sentences.romanized: Jigwon-eun haesanmul-gwa wanbyeokhage eoulliindago hamyeonseo, jeontong makgeolli-wa-eui peeoling-eul chucheonhae jueotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄴ/는다고 하다** indirect quotation: 어울린**다고 하면서** (while saying that it complements). The declarative quotative **-ㄴ/는다고** reports what someone said; combined with **-면서** it shows the staff member was saying this as she recommended: 맛있**다고 하면서** 내밀었다 (held it out while saying it was delicious). This is one of the most essential patterns for relaying speech naturally in Korean.
**-면서** simultaneous manner: 어울린다고 하**면서** 추천해 주었다 (recommended while saying it complemented). **-면서** here shows the verbal explanation and the physical recommendation happening together as a unified service act. The same subject (the staff member) performs both actions simultaneously — speaking and presenting the suggestion.

sentences.base_language: The first course arrived — a delicate seasonal dish with fresh garnishes and a light dressing.
sentences.target_language: 첫 번째 코스가 나왔다. 신선한 고명과 가벼운 드레싱을 곁들인 섬세한 제철 요리였다.
sentences.romanized: Cheot beonjjae koseu-ga nawatda. Sinseonhan gomyeong-gwa gabyeooun deuresing-eul gyeotdeullin semsehan jecheol yori-yeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**곁들이다** = to serve alongside, to accompany: 드레싱을 **곁들인** 요리 (a dish accompanied by dressing). **곁들이다** describes adding something as a side or accompaniment: 와인을 **곁들이다** (serve wine alongside), 소스를 **곁들이다** (serve with a sauce). As a past modifier **곁들인** it describes a noun that has the accompaniment as a feature — a very natural culinary expression in Korean.
**나오다** = to come out, to be served: 코스가 **나왔다** (the course came out / arrived). In a restaurant context **나오다** is the standard verb for food being brought to the table: 주문한 음식이 **나왔어요** (the food you ordered has come). Compare: **가져오다** (to bring — emphasises the action of the server), **나오다** (to come out — describes the dish's appearance from the kitchen's perspective, very natural in Korean).

sentences.base_language: Haeun took her first bite and felt a genuine wave of delight — it was one of the most delicious things she had ever eaten.
sentences.target_language: 하은은 첫 한 입을 먹어 보고는 진심 어린 감탄이 밀려오는 것을 느꼈다. 지금껏 먹어 본 것 중 가장 맛있는 음식 중 하나였다.
sentences.romanized: Haeun-eun cheot han ib-eul meogeo bogoneun jinsim eorin gamtan-i millyeooneun geot-eul neukkkyeotda. Jigeumkkeot meogeo bon geot jung gajang masitneun eumsik jung hana-yeotda.
literal: among the things [she] had tried eating
was one of the most delicious dishes.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어 보다** experiential: 먹어 **보고는** (tried eating), 먹어 **본** 것 (things [one] has tried/experienced eating). **-아/어 보다** expresses attempting or experiencing an action: 입어 **보다** (try on), 가 **보다** (try going). The past modifier **-아/어 본** then describes nouns in terms of past experience: 먹어 **본** 음식 (food one has eaten before). This is distinct from simply 먹**은** 음식 (food that was eaten), emphasising the experiential dimension.
**밀려오다** = to come flooding/washing in: 감탄이 **밀려오는** 것을 느꼈다 (felt a wave of delight wash in). **밀리다** (to be pushed/surged) + **오다** (to come): a vivid motion compound describing an emotion or sensation that arrives in a surge, like a wave: 그리움이 **밀려왔다** (a wave of longing washed over [me]), 피로가 **밀려오다** (exhaustion comes flooding in). It is far more evocative than the neutral 느끼다 alone.

sentences.base_language: The main course was a slow-braised dish with seasonal vegetables and a deep, rich sauce.
sentences.target_language: 메인 코스는 제철 채소와 깊고 진한 소스를 곁들여 천천히 조린 요리였다.
sentences.romanized: Mein koseu-neun jecheol chaeso-wa gipgo jinhan soseu-reul gyeotdeullyeo cheoncheonhi jorin yori-yeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-고** adjectival coordination: 깊**고** 진한 소스 (a deep and rich sauce). **-고** connects two descriptive verbs (adjectives) modifying the same noun: 크**고** 넓은 방 (a large and spacious room), 따뜻**하고** 부드러운 빛 (warm and soft light). Unlike **-면서** (simultaneous for action verbs), **-고** is the neutral coordinator for adjectives in a modifier chain.
**조리다** = to braise/simmer down, to reduce: 천천히 **조린** 요리 (a slowly braised dish). **조리다** (to braise, to simmer in sauce until reduced) differs from **끓이다** (to boil) and **굽다** (to grill/roast): it implies cooking in a flavored liquid over low heat until the liquid is absorbed and the dish becomes concentrated and rich. **천천히 조린** captures the slow, patient cooking process as a past modifier.

sentences.base_language: As they ate, Haeun and her friend talked and laughed freely, completely losing track of time.
sentences.target_language: 식사를 하면서 하은과 친구는 자유롭게 이야기하고 웃다가, 완전히 시간 가는 줄도 몰랐다.
sentences.romanized: Siksa-reul hamyeonseo Haeun-gwa chingu-neun jayuropge iyagihago utdaga, wanjeonhi sigan ganeun julddo molllatda.
literal: talked and laughed freely, and then (mid-action)
did not even know time was passing.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-다가** interruption or transition mid-action: 이야기하고 웃**다가** 시간을 잊었다 (were talking and laughing and [in the midst of that] lost track of time). **-다가** marks a situation where the first action was ongoing when a second event occurs or a transition takes place: 걷**다가** 넘어졌다 (was walking and then fell). Here it conveys the two friends were deep in conversation when, in the midst of that, time simply slipped away.
**-(으)ㄹ 줄 모르다** = to not realize/notice that: 시간 가는 **줄도 몰랐다** (didn't even realize time was passing). **-(으)ㄹ/는 줄** nominalises a state or fact; **모르다** (not know) applied to it means one fails to notice or realize it: 그가 올 **줄 몰랐다** (didn't know he would come). The addition of **도** (even) sharpens the sense of complete unawareness — they were so absorbed that the passage of time didn't register at all.

sentences.base_language: The staff member came back to check on them and Haeun praised the food with great enthusiasm.
sentences.target_language: 직원이 다시 와서 어떠냐고 물었을 때, 하은은 음식이 정말 훌륭하다고 열심히 칭찬했다.
sentences.romanized: Jigwon-i dasi waseo eotteonya-go mureosseul ttae, Haeun-eun eumsik-i jeongmal hullyunghada-go yeolsimhi chingchanhaetda.
literal: that the food was truly wonderful (quote)
sentences.grammarNote:
**-냐고 하다** indirect question quotation: 어떠**냐고** 물었다 (asked how it was). **-냐고** is the quotative ending for questions with descriptive verbs: 좋**냐고** 물었다 (asked if it was good), 힘드**냐고** 했다 (asked if it was hard). For action verbs the form is **-느냐고** or **-냐고**: 뭐 하**느냐고** 물었다 (asked what [I] was doing). These indirect question quotatives are essential for reporting others' enquiries.
**-(으)ㄴ/는다고 하다** declarative quotation: 훌륭하**다고** 칭찬했다 (praised [it], saying it was wonderful). **-다고** follows the plain present declarative form of adjectives (훌륭하**다고**) or action verbs (먹는**다고**) to report what was said. Here haeun's direct thought 「훌륭해요」 is transformed into reported speech: 훌륭하**다고** 말했다. This is one of the highest-frequency grammar patterns in Korean.

sentences.base_language: She smiled and said the chef would be very happy to hear that.
sentences.target_language: 직원은 미소를 지으면서 「셰프가 들으면 정말 기뻐할 거예요」라고 했다.
sentences.romanized: Jigwon-eun miso-reul jieumyeonseo 「syepeu-ga deureumyeon jeongmal gippewohal geoyeyo」rago haetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄹ 것이다** future inference/prediction: 기뻐**할 거예요** (will be happy / would be happy). **-(으)ㄹ 것이다** (contracted colloquially to **-(으)ㄹ 거다/거예요**) expresses a future expectation or inference: 비가 올 **거예요** (it will probably rain), 맛있을 **거예요** (it will be delicious). Inside the conditional **-면** clause the inference feels natural — 'if the chef hears, he will surely be happy.'
**미소를 짓다** = to make/form a smile: 미소를 **지으면서** (while smiling). **짓다** (to make/build/form) collocates with facial expressions and other intangibles: 표정을 **짓다** (to make an expression), 한숨을 **짓다** (to heave a sigh). **미소를 짓다** is more literary than simply 웃다 (to laugh/smile) — it emphasises the deliberate forming of a smile on the face, fitting the gracious service context.

sentences.base_language: For dessert, Haeun chose a warm traditional rice cake dessert served with sweet bean sauce.
sentences.target_language: 디저트로 하은은 달콤한 팥소를 곁들인 따뜻한 전통 떡 디저트를 골랐다.
sentences.romanized: Dijeoteu-ro Haeun-eun dalkomhan patso-reul gyeotdeullin ttatteuthan jeontong tteok dijeoteu-reul gollatda.
literal: a warm traditional rice cake dessert-OBJ
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)로** purpose/choice marker: 디저트**로** (for dessert / as dessert). **-(으)로** attached to a category noun expresses what something is chosen as or functions as: 선물**로** 샀다 (bought as a gift), 직업**으로** 삼다 (take as one's job). Here 디저트**로** frames the choice — 'as the dessert item' — rather than simply 디저트를 골랐다 (chose a dessert), which is also correct but less idiomatic for this framing.
**팥소** = sweet red bean paste/filling: one of the most iconic traditional Korean dessert ingredients. **팥** (red bean/adzuki) + **소** (filling). In dessert contexts **팥소** is a thick sweet paste; when served as a sauce it may be looser. The cultural specificity of this ingredient is worth noting — it grounds the dessert firmly in the Korean culinary tradition being celebrated throughout the story.

sentences.base_language: When the bill arrived, she was pleasantly surprised by how reasonable the price was for such quality.
sentences.target_language: 계산서가 나왔을 때, 하은은 이렇게 훌륭한 퀄리티에 이 정도 가격이라는 것이 기분 좋게 놀라웠다.
sentences.romanized: Gyesanseo-ga nawasseul ttae, Haeun-eun ireoke hullyunghan kweolliti-e i jeongdo gagyeok-iranneun geot-i gibun johge nollawotda.
literal: the fact that the price was this level-SUBJECT
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(이)라는 것** nominalised copula quotation: 가격**이라는 것이** 놀라웠다 (the fact that the price was [this level] was surprising). **-(이)라고 하다** is the quotative of the copula **이다**; nominalised with **-는 것** it creates a noun clause: 그가 학생**이라는 것** (the fact that he is a student). This pattern is used to comment on or evaluate a stated fact: 이것이 사실**이라는 것이** 믿기 어렵다 (it's hard to believe that this is true).
**기분 좋게** adverbial manner: **기분 좋게** 놀라웠다 (was surprisingly in a good-feeling way / pleasantly surprised). **기분 좋다** (to feel good) + **-게** (adverbial suffix turning an adjective into an adverb): the adverb modifies 놀라웠다 to specify the emotional quality of the surprise — positive, welcome. **-게** is the standard adverbial form: 조용**하게** (quietly), 빠르**게** (quickly), 기분 좋**게** (pleasantly).

sentences.base_language: They thanked the staff warmly and headed outside into the cool evening air.
sentences.target_language: 두 사람은 직원들에게 따뜻하게 감사 인사를 전하고는 서늘한 저녁 공기 속으로 걸어 나갔다.
sentences.romanized: Du saram-eun jigwondeul-ege ttatteutage gamsa insa-reul jeonhagoneun seoneulhan jeonyeok gongi sog-euro georeo nagatda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**인사를 전하다** = to convey/pass on greetings: 감사 **인사를 전했다** (conveyed their thanks). **전하다** (to convey/transmit) + **인사** (greeting/farewell): a more deliberate and warm expression than simply 감사했다 (thanked). **인사를 전하다** implies the thanks were meaningfully communicated, not just uttered: 안부를 **전하다** (pass on regards), 감사의 마음을 **전하다** (convey feelings of gratitude).
**걸어 나가다** = to walk out: **걸어 나갔다** (walked out/headed outside). **걸어** (walking — gerund of 걷다) + **나가다** (to go out): a compound motion verb common in Korean narrative. Korean frequently specifies the manner of movement (걸어, 뛰어, 날아) before the directional verb: 뛰어 **나가다** (run out), 달려 **들어오다** (run in). This granularity of motion description is a characteristic feature of Korean storytelling.

sentences.base_language: Outside, both friends agreed without hesitation that it had been a truly memorable evening.
sentences.target_language: 밖으로 나오자마자, 두 친구는 주저 없이 정말 잊지 못할 저녁이었다고 입을 모았다.
sentences.romanized: Bak-euro naojamaja, du chingu-neun jujeo eopsi jeongmal itji mohal jeonyeok-ieotdago ib-eul moatda.
literal: as soon as they came outside,
that it had truly been an unforgettable evening (quote)
sentences.grammarNote:
**-자마자** = as soon as, the moment: 나오**자마자** (the moment they came out). **-자마자** is the most immediate of the 'as soon as' patterns: the second action follows the first with zero delay. Compare: **-자** (as soon as — literary, slight pause possible), **-자마자** (the very instant — emphatic immediacy). It is common in narrative to dramatise the spontaneous agreement: they didn't even need to be outside for a moment.
**입을 모으다** = to speak in unison, to agree unanimously: **입을 모았다** (gathered their mouths / spoke as one). An idiomatic expression: **입** (mouth) + **모으다** (to gather/collect). It implies that everyone present voiced the same opinion: 모두 입을 **모아** 칭찬했다 (everyone praised in unison). The image of 'gathering mouths' evokes a chorus of agreement — a vivid idiomatic alternative to simply 동의했다 (agreed).

sentences.base_language: Haeun's feet ached pleasantly — they had walked through the old palace district before dinner.
sentences.target_language: 하은의 발은 기분 좋게 욱신거렸다. 저녁 식사 전에 오래된 궁궐 지구를 걸어 다녔던 것이다.
sentences.romanized: Haeun-eui bal-eun gibun johge ukssingeoryeotda. Jeonyeok siksa jeon-e oraedoen gunggwol jigu-reul georeo dannyeotdeon geot-ida.
literal: it was that [they] had been walking around.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-았/었던 것이다** retrospective explanation: 걸어 다녔**던 것이다** (it was that [they] had been walking around — that explains it). This pattern combines **-았/었던** (past-retrospective modifier) + **것이다** (it is the case that) to provide an explanation for a current state by pointing to a prior event: 늦었**던 것이다** (it was because [he] had been late — that's why). It has an explanatory, 'ah, that's because…' nuance very common in Korean reflection.
**욱신거리다** = to throb, to ache (pleasantly or painfully): 발이 **욱신거렸다** (the feet throbbed/ached). **욱신** is an onomatopoeic/mimetic root describing a pulsing, throbbing ache; **-거리다** makes it a verb: 머리가 **욱신거리다** (the head throbs). The modifier 기분 좋게 (pleasantly) reframes what could be discomfort as a satisfying physical reminder of a good day — a subtle but expressive detail.

sentences.base_language: They decided to take a taxi home rather than walk, feeling too satisfied to rush anything.
sentences.target_language: 걸어가기보다는 택시를 타고 가기로 했는데, 너무 만족스러워서 서두르고 싶지 않았던 것이다.
sentences.romanized: Georeo gagi-bodaneun taeksi-reul tago gagiро haetneunde, neomu manjokseureowoseo seodureugo sipji anatdeon geot-ida.
literal: decided to take a taxi and go-and,
it was that [they] had not wanted to rush.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-기보다는** comparative: 걸어가**기보다는** 택시를 탔다 (took a taxi rather than walking). **-기** nominalises the verb; **보다는** (than — comparative) + **는** (topic, adding contrast): together **-기보다는** elegantly sets up the rejected alternative before stating the chosen one. Compare: **대신에** (instead of — substitution), **-기보다** (rather than doing — comparison of actions).
**-아/어서** cause: 만족스러**워서** 서두르고 싶지 않았다 (didn't want to rush because [they were] too satisfied). **-아/어서** is the causal connector for internal states and reasons: 피곤**해서** 집에 갔다 (went home because [I] was tired). It is the most natural cause connector when the reason is a state or condition. Combined with **싶지 않았던 것이다** it gives the sentence a reflective, self-explanatory tone — 'that's simply how content they felt.'

sentences.base_language: In the taxi, Haeun was already looking up the restaurant on her phone, quietly planning her next visit.
sentences.target_language: 택시 안에서 하은은 벌써 핸드폰으로 그 레스토랑을 검색하고 있었는데, 다음 방문을 조용히 계획하고 있었던 것이다.
sentences.romanized: Taeksi an-eseo Haeun-eun beolsseo haendeuponeuro geu reseutorang-eul geomsaekago isseotneunde, daeum bangmun-eul joyonghi gyehoekago isseotdeon geot-ida.
literal: it was that [she] had been quietly planning.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-고 있다** progressive: 검색하**고 있었**는데 (was searching), 계획하**고 있었**던 것이다 (had been planning). **-고 있다** is the Korean progressive, describing an ongoing action at a reference time: 지금 뭐 하**고 있어요**? (What are you doing right now?). In past narrative **-고 있었다** sets the ongoing background action: she was searching — and in the midst of that searching, she was also already planning.
**벌써** = already (sooner than expected): **벌써** 검색하고 있었다 (was already searching). **벌써** carries a nuance of 'sooner than one might expect' or 'as early as now': 벌써 다 먹었어? (Did you eat it all already?). It adds a gently charming character note — the meal is barely over and Haeun's mind has already moved to the next visit, conveying her delight without stating it explicitly.
